speaker: interviewer
text: Gershkowitz, G-E-R-S-H-K-O-W-I-T-Z, this is roll two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saul, we were talking about you laying in the fields, when the Germans-- when the Russians ran and the Germans came in. Tell me exactly what happened. Did you see-- when did you see your first German?

speaker: interviewee
text: First German we saw first thing in the morning. When the sun came up, we saw them. They come in with horse and wagon, with the cannons and everything, going through the city. And then we saw a lot of-- a few German soldiers with black raincoats, which they were the ones who started to organize the ghetto for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now you were on the outskirts of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in the city. We-- we ran out from the house, and we laid in the garden-- from our-- in our garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and how close was that to the center of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the center-- the city wasn't too big. Most of the city was maybe one and a half-- one to one and a half kilometer. It wasn't big. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep going, we'll continue. The-- yeah, that's another car, sorry. The Germans came through, and had you, and your family, and friends-- other people-- gathered in the center just because you didn't have any place to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just came out. Nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you naturally--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that we went in to-- my father knew some people, which they had a big house and we went in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish people. Because most of the Jews were in the city. I would say 90% of this city-- proper-- was Jewish people. And we went in there, we didn't have no belongings, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just clothing on your back.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the back, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And continue, Saul.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they started to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] first they sent for the eldest of the people, whatever was left of us, to-- to speak to the Germans. And they wanted to make a-- a-- like Judenstaat, so people should-- if the Germans wanted to speak, they should have somebody to speak to.

speaker: interviewer
text: A representative.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and they had-- I think they had five people chosen. And then the five people made a Jewish police, which they were under the jurisdiction of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why did they say that was necessary?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was necessary because if they wanted to have 100 people to go to a field to work, they told the-- the Jews which were elected in the-- to run the thing. And then-- then some people didn't want to go, so the police-- they had arm bands as a police, with a-- with a star-- that they made-- made them go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family know anybody on the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know everybody, because everybody knew everybody at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family was sent out to do various tasks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was still-- at that time he was working for people for Ukraine. People about 15 or 17 miles from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: As what?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a carpenter. As a carpenter, because the people knew somebody in the city hall, and they made him go and do it. And that's how I am survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was allowed by the Germans and Jewish police to go, every day--

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to work, yeah, and come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get there, by cart?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the men he worked for, they used to come by with a horse and wagon, and they used to take him. And that particular time, I went with him a couple of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother, and your father's sister, and all the children, what did they do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was in the house. My sister used to go to work in the fields, or in the roads. Whatever there was to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things were frightening because Berezne had burned, but no danger yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no danger in the beginning, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you first sense--

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard that there's a lot killings and pogrom like they can catch 20, 30 people and shoot, because somebody hurt a German, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hear this, Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- you heard. Some radios were there, hidden, which people listened to. And you couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that ever happened in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pogrom, was there ever a pogrom in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then was there ever one after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a pogrom. This was right before the Jewish holidays. And I remember being with my dad about 15 miles away. He was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me, because I was still young and they didn't take me to work. So he took me so he can send me back with the men who he was working for, to bring some bread and vegetables home. Which, when I went with him they never searched the wagon. And this particular day I set out in the morning to bring some bread and something to my mother, and I saw a lot of people marching from the-- from the field, before we come into the city. Marching-- Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you came into--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the city, yeah. So the man who-- who I was riding with told me, he said, every time you see somebody just turn the head-- turn yourself away from him, and I'll see if I can do something, because they are killing the Jews in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's clarify this for just a minute. This gentleman that you were riding with, he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people that were in the fields--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were Jews, marched by very few Germans, most of them Ukrainian police.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were marching--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were marching to-- and there were three graves dug, which I find out later, and they were-- they were shooting them and putting them into the grave. They went in the grave, they shoot them, and then other people went in. And that's how they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were the men of Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about 3,400 people, or 3,500 people. They killed them in one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: 3,400 or 500-- 3,500 people. The men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Men, women, children, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Children as well. They took them from the city--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the city. They marched them, and they told them to take their belongings, whatever, because they're going to work in a camp. And instead of that, they marched them down to the field, and they told them to undress, and they shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The men, women, and children. But surely, you must have thought of your own family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure I did. But I was positive that they wouldn't kill them, because my dad made a couple of hiding places, and he divided family. He took my mother and my sister and put them in one hiding place, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house where we lived there, in the ghetto, he made a disappearing stairway-- which it went down in the cellar-- and he camouflaged it like you couldn't find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, he was a carpenter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and-- and my brother was in another place where they used to hide-- they used to store potatoes in the wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was your mother-- how many were in one, and how many were in another?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother my sister, Chana, was in one place. And my brother, Julius, was in another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone. And my-- and another two fellows, which was no relatives. And my sister-- older sister and my nephew were-- run away two weeks ago, because they knew something's going to happen. So my dad send her away to a place by the name of Mokwin, a small little-- we call it a [NON-ENGLISH], which it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: A hamlet?

speaker: interviewee
text: A hamlet, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they killed her. They find her, and they killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Mokwin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mokwin, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came back into Berezne with this man, and the wagon, and some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through. I went-- I saw the people were-- were walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Opposite.

speaker: interviewee
text: Opposite. All the Ukrainian-- big, you know, the doctors, the pharmacist, I went to the pharmacy place-- they were standing outside and looking. And every time I saw somebody I turned over, they shouldn't see me. And he took me out on the other side of the city and he told me, run. Don't look back, run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you want to see your mother? How old were you, 15? 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: But didn't you want to go back into Berezne to look--

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me-- because I knew that my sister is in Mokwin. And we knew that my dad told him if something happens, you should go to Mokwin. He knew somebody there which they would hide us out. So I ran to Mokwin. I didn't know how, but I ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this man's name, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: That allowed you to escape. He was Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ran to Mokwin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran to Mokwin that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month are we in, Saul? I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in August or September, one of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of '41, yes. Of, no, '42-- '42. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- I ran. And then somebody saw me running and they started to go after me. And there was a ditch on the bank of the river, and I went in in the sand underneath, and I-- and--and they couldn't-- they couldn't see me. And I stayed there until late at night when it was good and dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hidden in the sand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, late at night until I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What river, Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sluch, S-L-U-C-H, Sluch. Which it went into the-- to the Wisla. And I started to walk. I didn't know where I'm going, but I walked. And that woman stopped me. This was maybe 11 o'clock at night-- beautiful moon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, where are you going? Are you Jewish? I said, yes. I said, I don't know-- I-- I thought that I have a sister in here, which I would like to find her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this woman, I find out later that she used to be-- used to live with a Jewish man.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Gentile, and she had a Jewish man as a friend. And she told me, why don't you stay with me until the morning and I'll find out where your sister is. So I said, fine. What am I going to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you afraid of her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, when you're drowning and you don't know how to swim, you catch a straw. And she made a hole in-- in a big haystack. The haystack is maybe 8 or 9 feet high, about 8 feet diameter. And she dug a big hole there, and she put me in there, and she said you stay there and I'll give you-- And she had me there about a day and a half. She gave me two meals a day. And when they finished killing, they started--

speaker: interviewer
text: When who finished killing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, when they finished killing all the Jews down there, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berezne. They knew that there's a lot of hideouts which Jews hid. So the police were still there, the Jewish police. So the second day they took out my mother. Somebody-- some Jewish guy, man, thought that if he's going to tell where my mother, my sister, whatever is, they'll spare his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took out my mother with my sister, and when my mother went down the steps where she was coming, she knew where my brother was, and she says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. What does that mean in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be well, because I'm already gone. And that night, my brother, Julius, who is still alive, ran out in the middle of the night. And-- and he went the same path that I was going, and the same woman was there-- the same-- the third night. And she saw my brother walking-- we looked alike-- and she said, what's your name? He said, I'm Julius Gershkowitz. So she said, I have one of your brothers here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saul, do you know what happened to your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was killed with my-- with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken to the graves there, and they shot her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into the open grave? How do you know that, Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I know because I've been told. And I know that my father was alive, and he was in the woods after-- about six or eight months after they killed. He didn't know that we were alive, and one day, they-- he gave himself up.

speaker: interviewer
text: He gave himself up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because knowing that they killed his wife and the kids, he gave himself up.

speaker: interviewer
text: He thought all the children had been killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and they made him dig a grave, because the people told me about it-- were with him together-- are still in Israel now. I spoke to them in 1968, and they said they know that he was alive with them, and he couldn't stand being alone while his family was killed. He dug himself-- they made him dig a grave, and they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he fell into his own grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was about 52 or 54 years old I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did those people who ended up in Israel, that saw this, how did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of them went to the Russian underground-- Russian-- went underground in the woods. Some of them joined the Russian partisans, and I met a few guys, which they were with him in the Russian partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman who put you into the haystack, do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the name. A beautiful face, a nice zaftig woman, and very good natured woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I think that if they find out that one of them harbored Jews, they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on the outskirts of that town that your sister fled to. What was the name of that town again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just told you, Mokwin.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on the outskirts of Mokwin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's about six or seven miles from Berezne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how close to Mokwin was this haystack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was in Mokwin. It wasn't far from river. She lived almost-- not far from the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after the day and a half when you were there, and along came your brother, what did you do there? And this was very hot, wasn't it? It was August.

speaker: interviewee
text: She let us stay there for about four days. And then she came down one night and she said, people are talking that I am hiding Jews. ' I would say you should go away. And we went away. And she gave us some food and a jacket or something. And then when we went out there was a big church with the-- with the-- was standing on pillars. You know, the church was-- there was no cellar, but it was open. And we went in there-- figured to hide there for a while. And we met up with a Russian soldier, a deserter.

speaker: interviewer
text: A deserter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he thought that we have gold or money, because he knew that we were Jewish, and I think he wanted to take us both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he assume that all Jews had gold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then what happened is, in the middle of the day, we started to run. I said to Julius, I said, let's get out of here because he's going to kill us, or the Germans are going to kill us, or the Ukrainians are going to kill us. And we went to the-- to the water, to the Sluch, and it's a good thing it wasn't too wide at that place. And we swam over. He was shooting with his rifle.

speaker: interviewer
text: At you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At us, and he didn't hit us. And when we came out on the other side of the Sluch, of the river, there was the woods already, and we started to walk in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And at night, while we walked, we met up with another six of Jewish people, which they ran away from that pogrom. And we were there, at that time, for the fourth day we were about, I would say, 15 Jews. Three sisters, which one of them is married to my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Three sisters, two men, and a couple of married couples. And then we started to walk, because we hear there is partisans in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as you knew, you were walking east.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and we were-- we started waking for a few days, sleeping in the grass, and finding whatever we can. We stole potatoes in the-- in the ground. And at night, we used to make a big hole and make a small little fire to-- to cook the potatoes. The water was a creek water. One day I was drinking the water, and I saw little bugs in there. But if you want to drink, you drink. And we lived in the woods, and then we met some more people. By the time-- after a couple of weeks, we were about 30 Jews together.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: From different towns, or from Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from Berezne. Maybe a few of them was from a different city, but most of them from Berezne. Which, we knew each other. And at that time, we used to steal whatever we could at night in farms. A chicken, whatever we could. And we hear that if they catch you-- the Ukrainian people, if they bring a Jew to the authorities they used to pay him with a pound of salt, because salt was very, very scarce in there. So at that time, they used to pay a pound of salt for a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was worth it to the Ukrainians to look for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not all of them were bad, but there was some of them which were very bad. And at that point, we heard that the partisans are not far from us. And finally, after three or four weeks-- I think four weeks-- we met up with the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were speaking Russian. They were an outfit-- came down from Moscow. Because when were there for about a month or so with them, I remember we came down-- they made two big fires on each side for about 500 feet, and we had a plane from Moscow come down and bring some ammunition and a doctor, and take some-- they had a couple of wounded people to take back.

speaker: interviewer
text: These partisans, were they originally soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were soldiers, trained as partisans, and parachute down to the Ukraine and to arrest, and do whatever they can there to the Germans. We were with them for over eight months, until they took a lot-- they took in a lot of people at the time-- kids, women with children-- and they couldn't operate whatever they had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took all the men-- able men-- about 12 or 14 of us, and then all the women and kids, and they gave us rifles, each one, each man. And they said that you should do by yourself, because we can't do, and they-- we-- they separated us, and the kids, and the women, themselves. And they went their own way until the liberation when the Russians came in. Now, this was 1943. This would have, by now, brought you well into the spring of '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- was there a leader to that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: To our group?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had-- the elder-- the older man was the leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the partisan group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the partisan, yes. The name was Medvedev-- Medvedev. It's like, a bear in Russia is Medvedev. And he was a very handsome, nice man. Was very-- he was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he been in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a colonel in the military. I know, because when he-- when, after the war, we knew about it, because one of the women from our city-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH], who was there, has-- she was very friendly with him. And when he left she couldn't go with him because they told her that he's married. But then she went to Russia--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were lovers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went back to Russia, and she stayed with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She stayed with her father and two brothers. Her father died, but they came to Israel. And I met her in Boston, I think in 1960, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think '60, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was sort of a hero to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time he was a hero, yes. Yes, I think so. And he spared a lot of Jewish lives. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has he ever been-- have you ever told anybody at Yad Vashem about him? Would you consider him among the Righteous Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among the Righteous Gentiles, I consider the woman who saved my life, the man who took me through the city while the pogrom was going. About the righteous-- him, I consider, yes. Because if he would have wanted, he was the commander, he didn't have to have kids in an outfit like this, where--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he knew you were all Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, he knew. All Jews, yes, and they had Jewish partisans there from Russia. There were a few Jews who were Russian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incidentally, the Russians Jews also spoke Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little Yiddish, but mostly Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Yiddish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you feel rather warm towards them when you met them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we though that Messiah came when we saw them first, because you could sleep, you could make a tent, you could relax, you could make a fire at night, you could laugh. You felt like you were human again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children were, I suppose, trained? Or were they trained to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were trained, and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quiet, yes, they had to be quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Saul, thank you very much for that. We'll continue on tape three.
